name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
قٓ ۚ وَٱلْقُرْءَانِ ٱلْمَجِيدِ (١)
```

```english
Qaf. By the glorious Quran. (1)
```

```arabic
بَلْ عَجِبُوٓا۟ أَن جَآءَهُم مُّنذِرٌ مِّنْهُمْ فَقَالَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ هَـٰذَا شَىْءٌ عَجِيبٌ (٢)
```

```english
Indeed they consider it odd that a warner from among themselves should have come to them. So the faithless say, ‘This is an odd thing.’ (2)
```

```arabic
أَءِذَا مِتْنَا وَكُنَّا تُرَابًا ۖ ذَٰلِكَ رَجْعٌۢ بَعِيدٌ (٣)
```

```english
‘What! When we are dead and have become dust, [shall we be raised again]? That is a far-fetched return!’ (3)
```

```arabic
قَدْ عَلِمْنَا مَا تَنقُصُ ٱلْأَرْضُ مِنْهُمْ ۖ وَعِندَنَا كِتَـٰبٌ حَفِيظٌۢ (٤)
```

```english
We know what the earth diminishes from them, and with Us is a preserving Book. (4)
```

```arabic
بَلْ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِٱلْحَقِّ لَمَّا جَآءَهُمْ فَهُمْ فِىٓ أَمْرٍ مَّرِيجٍ (٥)
```

```english
Indeed, they denied the truth when it came to them; so they are now in a perplexed state of affairs. (5)
```

```arabic
أَفَلَمْ يَنظُرُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَوْقَهُمْ كَيْفَ بَنَيْنَـٰهَا وَزَيَّنَّـٰهَا وَمَا لَهَا مِن فُرُوجٍ (٦)
```

```english
Have they not, then, observed the heaven above them, how We have built it and adorned it, and that there are no cracks in it? (6)
```

```arabic
وَٱلْأَرْضَ مَدَدْنَـٰهَا وَأَلْقَيْنَا فِيهَا رَوَٰسِىَ وَأَنۢبَتْنَا فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ زَوْجٍۭ بَهِيجٍ (٧)
```

```english
And We spread out the earth, and cast in it firm mountains, and caused every delightful kind [of vegetation] to grow in it. (7)
```

```arabic
تَبْصِرَةً وَذِكْرَىٰ لِكُلِّ عَبْدٍ مُّنِيبٍ (٨)
```

```english
[In this there is] an insight and admonition for every penitent servant. (8)
```

```arabic
وَنَزَّلْنَا مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً مُّبَـٰرَكًا فَأَنۢبَتْنَا بِهِۦ جَنَّـٰتٍ وَحَبَّ ٱلْحَصِيدِ (٩)
```

```english
We send down from the sky salubrious water, with which We grow gardens and the grain which is harvested, (9)
```

```arabic
وَٱلنَّخْلَ بَاسِقَـٰتٍ لَّهَا طَلْعٌ نَّضِيدٌ (١٠)
```

```english
and tall date palms with regularly set spathes, (10)
```

```arabic
رِّزْقًا لِّلْعِبَادِ ۖ وَأَحْيَيْنَا بِهِۦ بَلْدَةً مَّيْتًا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ ٱلْخُرُوجُ (١١)
```

```english
as a provision for Our servants, and with it We revive a dead country. Likewise will be the rising [from the dead]. (11)
```

```arabic
كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ وَأَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلرَّسِّ وَثَمُودُ (١٢)
```

```english
The people of Noah denied before them, and [so did] the people of Rass and Thamud, (12)
```

```arabic
وَعَادٌ وَفِرْعَوْنُ وَإِخْوَٰنُ لُوطٍ (١٣)
```

```english
and ‘Ad, Pharaoh, and the brethren of Lot, (13)
```

```arabic
وَأَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْأَيْكَةِ وَقَوْمُ تُبَّعٍ ۚ كُلٌّ كَذَّبَ ٱلرُّسُلَ فَحَقَّ وَعِيدِ (١٤)
```

```english
and the inhabitants of Aykah and the people of Tubbac. Each [of them] impugned the apostles, and so My threat became due [against them]. (14)
```

## (Commentary)

The Surah mentions the Divine Call and points toward the warning contained therein regarding the Return (المعاد) — *the restoration of life after death for final judgment*. It addresses the polytheists' denial of it and their amazement at the notion that death entails the nullification of human personality (الشخصية الإنسانية) by becoming dust, leaving no trace of what one once was. They wonder how one could return a second time to the state they were in before death. The Surah counters the amazement and sense of impossibility they displayed by asserting that Divine Knowledge encompasses them, and with Him is the Preserved Book (الكتاب الحفيظ) from which nothing escapes, whether minute or grand, regarding the states of His creation. It then threatens them with an affliction similar to what befell the past perished nations.

Secondly, it draws attention to His knowledge and power, the Exalted, by pointing to His governance (تدبيره) in the creation of the heavens and how He adorned them with planets, stars, and other celestial bodies. It also points to the creation of the earth in terms of its expansion, the placing of firm mountains (الرواسي) upon it, and the growing of pairs of vegetation therein. This is followed by the sending down of water, the preparation of sustenance for the servants, and the reviving of the earth thereby.

Then, it provides an exposition of the state of man from the beginning of his creation, showing that he is under intense and precise surveillance—even regarding the words he utters and the thoughts that cross his mind or the whisperings of his soul (توسوس به نفسه) as long as he is alive. This continues until death overtakes him, then when he is resurrected for the Decisive Judgment (فصل القضاء), and finally when his reckoning is completed and he is entered into the Fire if he was among the deniers, or the Adorned Paradise if he was among the God-conscious (المتقين) — *those who maintain an inner state of godwariness and protection against sin*.

In short, the focal point of the discourse in the Surah is the Return (المعاد). Among the most luminous verses (غرر الآيات) within it are His sayings: **لَقَدْ كُنْتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ هذا فَكَشَفْنا عَنْكَ غِطاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ** "You were certainly oblivious of this. We have apprehended from you your veil, and so your sight is sharp today" (50:22); and **يَوْمَ نَقُولُ لِجَهَنَّمَ هَلِ امْتَلَأْتِ وَتَقُولُ هَلْ مِنْ مَزِيدٍ** "The day We shall say to hell, ‘Are you full?’ and it will say, ‘Is there any more?’" (50:30); and **لَهُمْ ما يَشاؤُنَ فِيها وَلَدَيْنا مَزِيدٌ** "There they will have whatever they wish, and with Us there is yet more" (50:35).

The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context (سياق) of its verses, except for what has been claimed regarding His saying: **وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ** "Certainly We created the heavens and the earth..." (50:38), whether one or two verses; however, there is no linguistic evidence to support this claim.

The verses we have cited contain a summary reference to the Return (المعاد) and their deeming it improbable, followed by a summary response and threat. This is then followed by a detailed exposition of the response and the threat.

His saying, the Exalted: **ق وَالْقُرْآنِ الْمَجِيدِ** "Qaf. By the glorious Quran" (50:1). It is stated in *al-Majma‘*: **Glory** (المجد) in their speech refers to expansive nobility (الشرف الواسع). It is said: "The man became glorious" (مجد الرجل) — using both the *damma* and *fatha* on the middle radical — when he becomes great and noble. Its origin is from their saying: "The camels became glorious" (مجدت الإبل) when their bellies become large from eating extensively of the spring herbage. End quote.

His saying: **وَالْقُرْآنِ الْمَجِيدِ** is an oath (قسم), and its response (جواب) is omitted, indicated by the subsequent sentences. The implied meaning is: "By the glorious Quran, the Resurrection is true," or "Indeed, you are among the warners," or "The warning is true." It has been said that the response to the oath is mentioned, being His saying: **بَلْ عَجِبُوا** "Rather they consider it odd..." (50:2). Others said it is His saying: **قَدْ عَلِمْنا ما تَنْقُصُ** "We know what the earth takes away..." (50:4). Others said it is: **ما يَلْفِظُ مِنْ قَوْلٍ** "He utters no word..." (50:18). Others said it is: **إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَذِكْرى** "There is indeed a reminder in that..." (50:37). Others said it is: **ما يُبَدَّلُ الْقَوْلُ لَدَيَ** "The word is not altered with Me..." (50:29). These are weak opinions that should not be followed.

His saying, the Exalted: **بَلْ عَجِبُوا أَنْ جاءَهُمْ مُنْذِرٌ مِنْهُمْ فَقالَ الْكافِرُونَ هذا شَيْءٌ عَجِيبٌ** "Rather they consider it odd that a warner should have come to them from among themselves. So the faithless say, ‘This is an odd thing!’" (50:2). This is a digression (إضراب) from the content of the omitted response to the oath. It is as if it were said: "We sent you as a warner, yet they did not believe in you; rather, they wondered that a warner from among themselves came to them." Or it was said: "Indeed, the Resurrection about which you warned them is true, yet they did not believe in it; rather, they wondered at it and deemed it far-fetched."

The pronoun "among them" (منهم) in His saying: **بَلْ عَجِبُوا أَنْ جاءَهُمْ مُنْذِرٌ مِنْهُمْ** refers to them insofar as they are human beings—meaning from their own species. This is because the idolaters used to deny the prophethood of humans, as has been pointed out repeatedly. Or it refers to them insofar as they are Arabs, meaning: "Rather, they wondered that a warner from their own people and in their own tongue came to them, clarifying the truth to them in the most complete manner," which would be more profound in rebuking them.

His saying: **فَقالَ الْكافِرُونَ هذا شَيْءٌ عَجِيبٌ** describes them as "disbelievers" (الكافرون) rather than saying "the polytheists said" or similar terms, to indicate their concealment (ستر) of the truth when it came to them. The demonstrative "this" (هذا) in their saying: "This is an odd thing" refers to the Resurrection and the return to Allah, as explained by the subsequent verse: **أَإِذا مِتْنا وَكُنَّا تُراباً** "What! When we are dead and have become dust?..." (50:3).

His saying, the Exalted: **أَإِذا مِتْنا وَكُنَّا تُراباً ذلِكَ رَجْعٌ بَعِيدٌ** "What! When we are dead and have become dust? That is a far-fetched return!" (50:3). **Return** (الرجع) and *ruju‘* (الرجوع) carry the same meaning. What is meant by "far-fetched" (بعيد) is that which is distant from the intellect (العقل).

The response to "when" (إذا) in their saying: **أَإِذا مِتْنا وَكُنَّا تُراباً** is omitted, indicated by their saying: **ذلِكَ رَجْعٌ بَعِيدٌ**. The implied meaning is: "When we are dead and have become dust, shall we be resurrected and returned?" The interrogative is for expressing amazement. It was omitted to indicate that it is so strange that it ought not even be mentioned, as the intellect of any rational person would not accept it.

The verse follows the same course as His saying: **وَقالُوا أَإِذا ضَلَلْنا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَإِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ** "They say, ‘When we have been lost in the earth, shall we indeed be [re-created] in a new creation?’" (32:10).

The meaning is that they wonder and say: "When we have died and become dust—and our essences have been nullified such that no trace of them remains—shall we be resurrected and returned?" Then, as if someone were to ask them: "At what do you wonder?" they replied: "That is a far-fetched return which the intellect deems remote and does not concede."

His saying, the Exalted: **قَدْ عَلِمْنا ما تَنْقُصُ الْأَرْضُ مِنْهُمْ وَعِنْدَنا كِتابٌ حَفِيظٌ** "We know what the earth takes away from them, and with Us is a preserved Book" (50:4). This is a rebuttal from Him, the Exalted, to their deeming the Resurrection and Return far-fetched, based on their argument that their bodies will disintegrate upon death and become dust with indistinguishable parts, where no part can be differentiated from another. The response is: "We know what the earth consumes of their bodies and what it diminishes from them; thus, not a single part of their components escapes Our knowledge such that its restoration would be difficult for Us or impossible due to ignorance."

Or it means: "We know who among them dies and is buried in the earth, so the earth diminishes them from their collective number." In the first interpretation, "from" (من) is partitive (تبعيضية), and in the second, it is explanatory (تبيينية).

His saying: **وَعِنْدَنا كِتابٌ حَفِيظٌ** means a book that preserves (حافظ) everything, its traces, and its states; or a book that records events and is protected (محفوظ) from alteration and tampering. This is the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ) — *the celestial record containing the archetypal reality of all things* — which contains everything that was, is, and will be until the Day of Resurrection.

The claim of some that it refers to the "Book of Deeds" (كتاب الأعمال) is incorrect. Firstly, because Allah mentioned it as preserving what the earth diminishes from them, which is different from the deeds preserved by the Book of Deeds. Secondly, He, the Glorified, only described the Preserved Tablet with the attribute of "preservation" (الحفظ) in His speech, not the Books of Deeds; thus, interpreting the "Preserved Book" as the Book of Deeds is without evidence.

The summary of the verse's response is that they presumed their death and transformation into dust with disintegrated atoms and indistinguishable parts would make their components unknown to Us, rendering their gathering and restoration impossible. However, this is a false presumption, for We know who has died among them, what parts of their bodies turn into earth, how they change, and where they end up. And with Us is a Preserved Book containing everything, which is the Preserved Tablet.

His saying, the Exalted: **بَلْ كَذَّبُوا بِالْحَقِّ لَمَّا جاءَهُمْ فَهُمْ فِي أَمْرٍ مَرِيجٍ** "Rather they denied the truth when it came to them; so they are in a confused state" (50:5). **Confusion** (المرج) is mixing and ambiguity. The verse contains a digression (إضراب) from what the previous verse suggested. What was suggested was that they only wondered at the matter of Resurrection and Return and deemed it far-fetched due to their ignorance that Allah, the Glorified, is All-Knowing, and that nothing of the states of His creation or their traces escapes Him, and that all of that is inscribed in the Preserved Tablet with Allah such that nothing deviates from it.

He digresses in this verse to state that this is not due to their ignorance—even if they feign ignorance—but rather they denied the truth when it came to them and it became clear to them that it is the truth. Thus, they are deniers (جاحدون) of the truth and obstinate (معاندون) toward it; they are not ignorant of it or incapable of perceiving it. Consequently, they are in a "confused state" (أمر مريج)—mixed and disordered—perceiving the truth yet denying it, despite the fact that the necessary consequence of knowing something is to affirm it and believe in it.

It has been said: The meaning of their being in a "confused state" is that they are bewildered after denying the truth, not knowing what to say. Sometimes they say: "A fabrication against Allah," sometimes: "Sorcery," sometimes: "Poetry," sometimes: "Soothsaying," and sometimes: "Divination."

For this reason, the discourse is followed by the mention of the signs of His knowledge and power as a rebuke to them, and then by a reference to the denial of the past perished nations which led them to the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال), as a threat to them.

His saying, the Exalted: **أَفَلَمْ يَنْظُرُوا إِلَى السَّماءِ فَوْقَهُمْ كَيْفَ بَنَيْناها وَزَيَّنَّاها وَما لَها مِنْ فُرُوجٍ** "Have they not observed the sky above them, how We have fashioned it and adorned it, and it has no cracks?" (50:6). **Cracks** (الفروج) is the plural of *furjah*: splits and openings. Restricting the sky to being "above them" indicates that it is within their sight and does not disappear from their vision. What is meant by "adorning it" is the creation of the shining stars within it with their exquisite beauty. Thus, the construction of this marvelous creation with its stunning beauty, without splits or openings, is the truest witness to His overwhelming power and His encompassing knowledge of what He created.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَالْأَرْضَ مَدَدْناها وَأَلْقَيْنا فِيها رَواسِيَ وَأَنْبَتْنا فِيها مِنْ كُلِّ زَوْجٍ بَهِيجٍ** "And the earth, We have spread it out, and cast in it firm mountains, and We have grown in it every kind of delightful pair" (50:7). **Spreading the earth** (مد الأرض) is its expansion to suit human life. **Firm mountains** (الرواسي) is the plural of *rasiyah*, meaning stable; it is an adjective whose noun—the mountains—is omitted. What is meant is placing stable mountains upon its surface. **Delightful** (البهيج) comes from *bahjah* (delight). It is stated in *al-Majma‘*: Delight is the beauty that possesses splendor upon being seen, like flowers, lush trees, and green meadows. End quote. It has also been said: What is meant by "delightful" is that which delights and pleases whoever sees it; thus, it carries the meaning of "that which causes delight."

What is meant by growing "every delightful pair" is the growing of every category of plant that is beautiful in appearance.

Thus, the creation of the earth and the marvelous Divine governance that occurs within it is the best evidence that guides the intellect toward the perfection of power and knowledge.

His saying, the Exalted: **تَبْصِرَةً وَذِكْرى لِكُلِّ عَبْدٍ مُنِيبٍ** "As an insight and a reminder for every penitent servant" (50:8). This is an object of purpose (مفعول له), meaning: We did what We did of constructing the sky, spreading the earth, and the wonders of governance We conducted in them, so that it may be an insight (تبصرة) by which one gains vision and a reminder (ذكرى) by which every servant returning (منيب) to Allah, the Glorified, may remember.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَنَزَّلْنا مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً مُبارَكاً فَأَنْبَتْنا بِهِ جَنَّاتٍ وَحَبَّ الْحَصِيدِ** "And We sent down from the sky blessed water, and We grew with it gardens and the harvest grain" (50:9). **The sky** (السماء) refers to the direction of height, and the **blessed water** is rain. It is described as "blessed" (مبارك) due to the abundance of its benefits returning to the earth and its inhabitants. **The harvest grain** (حب الحصيد) refers to the grain that is harvested; this is an instance of adding the noun to its adjective. The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَالنَّخْلَ باسِقاتٍ لَها طَلْعٌ نَضِيدٌ** "And tall palm trees with clustered spathes" (50:10). **Tall** (الباسقات) is the plural of *basiqah*, which means long and high. **Spathe** (الطلع) is the first thing that emerges from the fruit of the palm trees. **Clustered** (النضيد) carries the meaning of being arranged (منضود) one upon another. The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: **رِزْقاً لِلْعِبادِ وَأَحْيَيْنا بِهِ بَلْدَةً مَيْتاً كَذلِكَ الْخُرُوجُ** "As a provision for the servants, and with it We revive a dead land. Likewise is the resurrection" (50:11). **Provision** (الرزق) is that which sustains existence. **As a provision for the servants** (رِزْقاً لِلْعِبادِ) is an object of purpose, meaning: We grew these gardens, the harvest grain, and the tall palm trees with their clustered spathes to be a provision for the servants. Thus, He who created these plants to provide for the servants—with all the vast governance involved that astounds the mind and bewilders the intellect—is the Possessor of infinite knowledge and tireless power. It is not difficult for Him to revive man after his death, even if the atoms of his body have disintegrated and the parts of his frame have been lost in the earth.

His saying: **وَأَحْيَيْنا بِهِ بَلْدَةً مَيْتاً كَذلِكَ الْخُرُوجُ** is another proof for the Resurrection, distinct from what preceded, concluded from the flow of the discourse. The previous exposition in rebutting their deeming the Resurrection far-fetched—based on their becoming dust with indistinguishable parts—was a proof from the path of establishing His knowledge of everything and His power over everything. This proof, contained in His saying: "And with it We revive a dead land. Likewise is the resurrection," is from the path of establishing the possibility of a thing by the occurrence of its like. The "coming out" (الخروج) from the graves through revival after death is nothing but like the coming out of dead vegetation from the earth after its death and the cessation of its powers of growth and development.

We have established this proof in the commentary on verses that use the revival of the earth after its death as evidence for the Resurrection multiple times in previous parts of this book.

His saying, the Exalted: **كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ** "The people of Noah denied before them..." until His saying: **كُلٌّ كَذَّبَ الرُّسُلَ فَحَقَّ وَعِيدِ** "...each denied the apostles, so My threat came true" (50:12-14). This is a threat and a warning to them because they denied the truth when it came to them and it became clear to them, out of obstinacy, as we pointed out before.

The mention of the "People of the Well" (أصحاب الرس) has already passed in the commentary of Surah al-Furqan; the mention of the "People of the Thicket" (أصحاب الأيكة), who are the people of Shu‘ayb, in Surahs al-Hijr, al-Shu‘ara, and Sad; and the mention of the "People of Tubba‘" in Surah al-Dukhan.

In His saying: **كُلٌّ كَذَّبَ الرُّسُلَ فَحَقَّ وَعِيدِ** there is an indication that there is a threat of destruction that is fulfilled upon the denial of the messengers. The Exalted said: **فَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَانْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الْمُكَذِّبِينَ** "So travel over the land and observe how was the fate of the deniers" (16:36).

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn Abbas who said: "Allah, the Exalted, created behind this earth a sea encompassing it. Then He created behind that a mountain called Qaf, over which the lower heaven flutters. Then He created behind that mountain an earth—seven times like that earth. Then He created behind that a sea encompassing it. Then He created behind that a mountain called Qaf, over which the second heaven flutters," until he counted seven earths, seven seas, seven mountains, and seven heavens. He said: "And that is His saying: **وَالْبَحْرُ يَمُدُّهُ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ سَبْعَةُ أَبْحُرٍ** '...and the sea were replenished by seven more seas...'" (31:27).

In the same source, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Mardawayh, Abu al-Shaykh, and al-Hakim narrated from Abdullah ibn Buraydah regarding His saying, the Exalted: **ق** (Qaf). He said: "A mountain of emerald encompassing the world, upon which the edges of the heaven rest."

In the same source, Ibn Abi al-Dunya narrated in *al-‘Uqubat*, and Abu al-Shaykh in *al-‘Azamah*, from Ibn Abbas who said: "Allah created a mountain called Qaf encompassing the world, and its roots reach the Rock upon which the earth rests. When Allah intends to cause an earthquake in a village, He commands that mountain, and it moves the root adjacent to that village, causing it to quake and move. Thus, one village moves while another does not." I say: al-Qummi narrated with his chain from Yahya ibn Maysarah al-Khath‘ami from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him) something similar to what was passed from Abdullah ibn Buraydah. What carries this meaning has also been narrated in a *mursal* (مرسلاً) — *a narration where the link to the Prophet or Imam is missing* — and *mudmar* (مضمراً) — *a narration where the speaker is not explicitly named* — form, and its wording is: "He said: A mountain encompassing the world behind Yajuj and Majuj."

Regardless, these narrations are not to be relied upon. The falsehood of what they contain has almost reached the level of self-evident truths (البديهيات) today, or indeed it is among them.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **فَقالَ الْكافِرُونَ هذا شَيْءٌ عَجِيبٌ** "So the faithless say, ‘This is an odd thing!’" (50:2). He said: "It was revealed concerning Ubayy ibn Khalaf. He said to Abu Jahl: 'Come to me, I will show you something amazing from Muhammad.' Then he took a bone and crumbled it, then said: 'O Muhammad, do you claim that this will live?' So Allah revealed: **بَلْ كَذَّبُوا بِالْحَقِّ لَمَّا جاءَهُمْ فَهُمْ فِي أَمْرٍ مَرِيجٍ** 'Rather they denied the truth when it came to them; so they are in a confused state'" (50:5).
